coredata.dc:title: An off-on fluorescent probe based on graphene quantum dots intercalated hydrotalcite for determination of ascorbic acid and phytase
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Han
Chen, Ligang
Niu, Na
coredata.dc:description: A new off-on fluorescent probe was successfully synthesized and used to detect ascorbic acid and phytase. The composite material of hydrotalcite and graphene quantum dots were synthesized through citrate intercalated hydrotalcite using hydrothermal carbonization method. The layered structure of hydrotalcite can prevent the aggregation and promote the uniformity of graphene quantum dots. The off-on fluorescent probe detection mechanism is as follows. Fe3+ ions were firstly added into the solution of the composite material, and the fluorescence was quenched. Then, ascorbic acid was added into the fluorescence probe system, which reduced iron ions and turned on fluorescence to realize the detection of ascorbic acid. In addition, the reaction of phytase and l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate can produce ascorbic acid, further achieving the purpose of phytase detection. The concentration of the analyte and the recovery of the fluorescence intensity show a linear relationship, the linear ranges were 5−300 μmol L−1 and 0.1–4 U mL−1 for ascorbic acid and phytase, respectively. The recoveries of the experiment were 96.3 %–103.1 % and 95.4 %–104.9 %, respectively. The fluorescence method has the advantages of fast, stable, high sensitivity and high selectivity, which has been successfully applied to the determination of ascorbic acid in fruit juice and phytase in feed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrotalcite
Graphene quantum dots
Ascorbic acid
Phytase
Off-on

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of ascorbic acid and phytase
The pretreatment and detect process of the real sample
Optimization of detection conditions of LDH-GQDs fluorescent probe
Fluorescence detection of ascorbic acid and phytase
Possible mechanism of quenching of LDH-GQDs
Application of this method in real sample detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( B2008001 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2572017EB08 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team), Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund ( LBH-Z16009 ), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2016M591501 , 2017T100218 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM of LDH-GQDs. B) FTIR spectrum of the LDH-GQDs. C) XPS of the LDH-GQDs. D) The C1 s analysis and E) O1 s analysis of the LDH-GQDs. F) The XRD of the LDH-GQDs. G) AFM image of LDH-GQDs.

A) The fluorescence spectra of the LDH-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths. B) Stability of the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs (Ex = 350 nm, Em =454 nm). C) The photostability of LDH-GQDs and GQDs under different bleaching time.

A) The effect of ions on the fluorescence of LDH-GQDs. B) The fluorescence spectra of LDH-GQDs after reaction with a series of concentrations of Fe3+ solution (inset: The linear relationships for LDH-GQD and Fe3+). C) The change of the fluorescence intensity when the Fe3+ solution added to the solution of LDH-GQDs at different reaction times. D) The change of the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs + Fe3++ascorbic acid system at different reaction times. E) The effect of pH value on fluorescent intensity of the sensing system.

A) Fluorescence spectra of LDH-GQDs with the addition of different ascorbic acid concentrations. B) Stern–Volmer relationship between F0/F and ascorbic acid concentration. C) Fluorescence spectra of LDH-GQDs with the addition of different phytase concentrations. D) Stern–Volmer relationship between F0/F and phytase concentration.

A) Ultraviolet absorption spectrums of Fe3+solution (curve 1), Fluorescent excitation spectrum (curve 2) and fluorescence emission spectrum (curve 3) of LDH-GQDs. B) The fluorescence decay traces of LDH-GQDs at 454 nm emission wavelength before adding Fe3+ solution. C) The fluorescence decay traces of LDH-GQDs at 454 nm emission wavelength after adding Fe3+ solution. D) Ultraviolet absorption spectrums of LDH-GQDs, Fe3+, ascorbic acid and LDH-GQDs + Fe3++ ascorbic acid.
A) The influence of the interfering substances (The experiment of detecting ascorbic acid). B) The influence of the interfering substances (The experiment of detecting phytase).
The schematic illustration of the preparation procedure and possible sensing mechanism of LDH-GQDs fluorescence probe to ascorbic acid and phytase.
Detection and average recovery of ascorbic acid in juice with 50 μmol L−1 spiked.
Detection and average recovery of phytase in feed with 0.2 U mL−1 spiked.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of ascorbic acid.

row.entry: Acetic acid ultrasonic-assisted extraction and pulse microwave-extraction

head.ce:title: An off-on fluorescent probe based on graphene quantum dots intercalated hydrotalcite for determination of ascorbic acid and phytase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization,Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation procedure and possible sensing mechanism of LDH-GQDs fluorescence probe to ascorbic acid and phytase.

A new off-on fluorescent probe was successfully synthesized and used to detect ascorbic acid and phytase. The composite material of hydrotalcite and graphene quantum dots were synthesized through citrate intercalated hydrotalcite using hydrothermal carbonization method. The layered structure of hydrotalcite can prevent the aggregation and promote the uniformity of graphene quantum dots. The off-on fluorescent probe detection mechanism is as follows. Fe3+ ions were firstly added into the solution of the composite material, and the fluorescence was quenched. Then, ascorbic acid was added into the fluorescence probe system, which reduced iron ions and turned on fluorescence to realize the detection of ascorbic acid. In addition, the reaction of phytase and l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate can produce ascorbic acid, further achieving the purpose of phytase detection. The concentration of the analyte and the recovery of the fluorescence intensity show a linear relationship, the linear ranges were 5−300 μmol L−1 and 0.1–4 U mL−1 for ascorbic acid and phytase, respectively. The recoveries of the experiment were 96.3 %–103.1 % and 95.4 %–104.9 %, respectively. The fluorescence method has the advantages of fast, stable, high sensitivity and high selectivity, which has been successfully applied to the determination of ascorbic acid in fruit juice and phytase in feed.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid), also known as l-ascorbic acid, is an acidic polyhydroxy compound containing six carbon atoms and is abundant in fruits and vegetables. Ascorbic acid can regulate the redox and metabolism of human body. The lack or excess of ascorbic acid in human body will cause different diseases [1,2]. Therefore, it is very important to detect ascorbic acid quickly, accurately and sensitively. There are many methods for the detection of ascorbic acid, such as liquid chromatography [3], electrochemical methods [4] and so on.

Phytase is an enzyme used to eliminate anti-nutrients in plant-based feeds. It is a phosphomonoester hydrolase and is widely found in animals, plants and microorganisms. Phytase releases part of the bound nutrients, so that phosphorus and other substances can be used by livestock and poultry [5,6]. Phytase has very high application value in feeds. The commonly used method to detect phytase activity is spectrophotometric method, such as molybdenum yellow method [7] and ferrous sulfate molybdenum blue method [8]. In addition, high performance liquid chromatography [9], agar plate culture method [10] and biosensor [11] also were established to detect phytase. This work established a fluorescent method for detecting phytase, which aims to more stable, easy and rapid detection of phytase.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new type of nanomaterial with a size of less than 100 nm. One or more layers of graphene constitute graphene quantum dots, and some of its chemical bond groups are on its edges or surfaces [12–14]. GQDs have excellent quantum size effects, excellent solubility, low toxicity, simple fabrication and better binding ability with other molecules. Therefore, GQDs have great application prospects in biological detect [15–19]. Many studies have demonstrated the potential of GQDs as fluorescent probe materials in the detection of biological molecules [20–22], enzymes [23], medicine [24] and other analytes [25,26]. However, there are some weaknesses with GQDs, such as easy aggregation quenching, instability and so on.

Hydrotalcite and hydrotalcite like compounds are called layered double hydroxides (LDH). They have special physical and chemical properties with layered structure. Their lamellar spacing is nanometer. Due to the special structure of LDH, hydrotalcite was introduced to ameliorate the properties of fluorescent materials. Hydrotalcite can provide a limited and orderly microenvironment for intercalation molecules, reduce π-π accumulation, and inhibit thermal vibration of the polymer backbone, which can be effective improve the fluorescence properties of composite materials [27,28]. Dang et al. intercalated the fluorescent molecule 8-hydroxy-fluorene-1, 3, 6-tri transverse acid salt and surfactant into the zinc-aluminum hydrotalcite layer by co-precipitation [29]. The synthesized materials had two fluorescence emission peaks. This is due to the unique spatial structure of layered hydrotalcite and the addition of long chain surfactant to the layered hydrotalcite, which can isolate the fluorescent molecules and tune the luminescent properties of the material. Yan et al. assembled the p-conjugated polymers and rigid LDH monolayers alternately to obtain high inorganic/organic hybrid ultrathin films [30]. LDH monolayers can provide a more limited and ordered environment for the p-conjugated polymers and improve the stability of intercalation molecules.

In this work, citrate hydrotalcite were prepared using citrate intercalated magnesium aluminum hydrotalcite, and then a one-pot hydrothermal method was used to synthesize layered double hydroxide graphene quantum dot composite (LDH-GQDs). Graphene quantum dots synthesized by hydrotalcite confinement were not prone to aggregation, and have good fluorescence stability. Based on the synthesized composite, we have successfully constructed an “off-on” fluorescence probe for detect ascorbic acid and phytase (Scheme. 1 ). The structure and properties of the probe, as well as the reaction mechanism of fluorescence quenching, were investigated in detail. In addition, the selectivity and sensitivity detections of ascorbic acid and phytase in actual samples were also performed.

Sodium citrate, magnesium nitrate hexahydrate, aluminum nitrate hexahydrate, ammonia water, hydrochloric acid, acetic acid, sodium acetate, citric acid, ascorbic acid and Tris were got by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ferric chloride and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were got by Guangfu (Tianjin, China). l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate was obtained by Macklin (Beijing, China). Phytase were got by Yuanye (Shanghai, China). The high-purity water was obtained by the Milli-Q water system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).The reagents used are analytical grade.

LDH-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and improved on the basis of the literature [31]. First, 0.045 mol Mg(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.015 mol Al(NO3)3·9H2O were dissolved in 50 mL water, 0.008 mol sodium citrate was dissolved in 50 mL water, the mixed solution was added to the citrate solution under stirring, and the pH value was adjusted to 10.5 by adding NaOH solution. Next, mixed solution was stirred at 35 °C for 6 h. Then, the precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water, 12 mL of precipitate solution with concentration of 0.05 g mL-1 and 2 mL of ammonia water (95 %) were mixed and added into stainless steel autoclave, and the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 8 h. Finally, the reactants were centrifuged, washed with deionized water three times, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C to obtain the product. Citric acid (0.3 g) was dissolved in 12 mL water, then 2 mL of ammonia water (95 %) was added, and the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 8 h to obtain GQDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield of LDH-GQDs was calculated with excitation wavelength at 350 nm according to the equation.
Q
C D
=
Q R
∙
I
C D
I R
∙
A R
A
C D
∙
η
C D
2
η R 2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is fluorescence spectra intensity, A is the absorbance, η is the solvent refractive index. The subscript R designates reference in this equation. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution was selected as the reference [32].

The structure of the composite was characterized by FT-IR360 (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). The X-ray diffraction patterns of the composites were characterized by the Shimadzu XRD-600 diffractometer (Kyoto, Japan). The imaging results of materials were characterized byh7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The UV absorption spectrum of the composite was given by using the TU-1901 spectrometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China). Fluorescence lifetime was detected by the QuantaMaster 8000 Modular Fluorescence Spectrometer (Horiba, Canada). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was taken using the PicoPlus II AFM system (Molecular Imaging, USA). The intensity of fluorescence spectrum was recorded at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The fluorescence spectrum was scanned at 300−700 nm. The fluorescence emission peak is located at 454 nm. The fluorescence measurement parameters are: the photomultiplier tube voltage is 550 V; the gap width is 10 nm; the scanning speed is 240 nm min−1. The scan interval is 1 nm. Each sample was analyzed for three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ascorbic acid and phytase
ce:section.ce:para: For ascorbic acid detection, 300 μL of LDH-GQDs solution (200 μg mL−1), 600 μL of Tris HCl buffer solution (10 mmol L−1), 100 μL of Fe3+ solution (180 μmol L−1) and a series concentrations of ascorbic acid solutions were added to the centrifuge tube, reacted at room temperature for 20 min, and then the fluorescence detection was carried out.

For phytase detection, 10 mL solution of l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate (5 mmol L−1), 500 μL of acetic acid and a series of phytase solution were add to the centrifuge tube, the enzyme hydrolysis reaction was carried out in a water bath at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, 300 μL of LDH-GQDs solution (200 μg mL−1), 600 μL of Tris HCl buffer solution (10 mmol L−1), 100 μL of Fe3+ solution (180 μmol L−1) and products of phytase hydrolysis (500 μL) were added in turn, reacted at room temperature for 20 min, and then the fluorescence detection was carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The pretreatment and detect process of the real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid in fruit juice and phytase in feed were detected. The fruit juice (grape juice, apple juice, lemon juice and orange juice) was purchased from Harbin supermarket (China). Then, the juice was filtered with 0.45 μm membrane for three times, and was diluted with deionized water for 10 times respectively. For the experiment of detecting ascorbic acid in actual samples, the steps are consistent with the method for detecting ascorbic acid standard in Section 2.4. After the mixed solution including LDH-GQDs, Fe3+, Tris HCl buffer solution and actual samples was completely reacted, in order to remove the interference from the background fluorescence of the juice, the mixed solution was centrifuged and the supernatant was discarded, then the fluorescence probe adsorbed ascorbic acid was redispersed in Tris HCL buffer solution for fluorescence detection.

Four kinds of feed including fish feed, pig feed, chicken feed and cattle feed were purchased from a feed market in Harbin, China. The feed sample (5 g) and 100 mL of acetic acid buffer solution were added to the centrifuge tube, shaken at 200 r min−1 for 1 h at room temperature, and then centrifuged at 4000 r/min for 10 min, and the supernatant was taken. The detection steps of phytase in the actual sample are consistent with the methods for detecting phytase standard in Section 2.4. Similarly, in order to remove the interference from the background fluorescence of the feed, the mixed solution including LDH-GQDs, Fe3+, Tris HCl buffer solution and hydrolysis products of actual samples was centrifuged and the supernatant was discarded, then the fluorescence probe adsorbed ascorbic acid was redispersed in Tris HCL buffer solution for fluorescence detection.

Fig. 1 A is a TEM image of LDH-GQDs. It can be seen that the GQDs are dispersed and intercalated on the hydrotalcite. The hydrotalcite has a sheet-like structure, and the GQDs have a spherical structure with a diameter of about 5−6 nm.

Fig. 1B shows the FTIR spectra of the LDH-GQDs. It can be seen from the figure that the peak at 1386 cm−1 is the infrared characteristic peak of CO, the characteristic peak at 649 cm−1 is caused by metal oxygen metal stretching, and the characteristic peaks at 1624 cm−1 and 3446 cm−1 are the characteristic peaks derived from hydroxyl groups. Moreover, a new absorption band appears near 1075 cm−1, which is caused by the bending vibration of COC of graphene quantum dots, which indicates that GQDs are successfully inserted into the middle layer of hydrotalcite.

It can be seen from Fig. 1C that the LDH-GQDs mainly contain C1 s of 291.6 eV, O1 s of 539.2 eV, Al 2p of 81.1 eV, Mg 1 s of 1314.6 eV, and auger electron peaks of magnesium at 314.8 eV. As shown in the Fig. 1D, the carbon spectrum can be fitted to four small peaks, including C = C of 290.8 eV, C-C of 291.3 eV, C-O of 292.5 eV, and C = O of 295.1 eV. And the oxygen spectrum is divided into two small peaks are C−OH / C-O-C at 538.4 eV and C = O at 539.6 eV (Fig. 1E).

It can be seen from Fig. 1F that the XRD pattern of the synthesized LDH-GQDs have good crystallinity. The diffraction peaks 2θ of (003), (006), (009) are placed at 7.76°, 15.27°, and 22.56°. It shows a strong diffraction peak at the low 2θ position and it indicates that the synthesized LDH-GQDs have a well ordered structure [33].
Fig. 1G shows the AFM image and the result is about 15−20 nm. This may be the result of multilayer stacking of LDH-GQDs.

The fluorescence properties of LDH-GQDs are the key factor in the experiment. Fig. 2 A shows the fluorescence spectra of LDH-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. It can be seen that the position of emission wavelength does not change with the change of excitation wavelength, but the different excitation wavelength will affect the fluorescence intensity of emission wavelength. The fluorescence intensity is the strongest at 350 nm excitation wavelength. The experiment of fluorescence quantum yield of LDH-GQDs also was carried out and the measurement result was 11.65 %.

Fig. 2B shows the stability of the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs at 350 nm excitation and 454 nm emission wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs was measured once every 5 min for a period of time. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs had not changed much, indicating that our synthesized LDH-GQDs had good stability.

The change of fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs with bleaching time under UV irradiation was recorded, with the GQDs solution as a comparison sample. The Fig. 2C displayed that the photostability of LDH-GQDs was significantly improved. The reason may be that LDH provides a stable microenvironment for the interlayer fluorescent materials and effectively prevent the aggregation quenching of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions of LDH-GQDs fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the effect of various ions on the fluorescence of LDH-GQDs, the same concentration of Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Mg2+ and Al3+ was added and reacted with LDH-GQDs under the same experimental conditions. The Fig. 3 A shows that Hg2+ has weak quenching ability to fluorescent probe and Fe3+ has the best quenching effect on the fluorescence probe. So, Fe3+ was selected to turn off the fluorescence probe.

The effect of Fe3+ concentration on the quenching of LDH-GQDs was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of LDH-GQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ are shown in the Fig. 3B. As the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually decreases. When Fe3+ concentration range is 40−180 μmol L−1, it has a linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity. When the concentration of Fe3+ continues to increase, there is almost no significant change in the shape of the spectrum. Therefore, 180 μmol L−1 was selected for subsequent experiments.

The effects of reaction time on LDH-GQDs and Fe3+ were studied. From Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs with Fe3+ decreased rapidly within 4 min, and the fluorescence intensity remained stable after 5 min. Therefore, ascorbic acid was added to the system after the LDH-GQDs reacted with Fe3+ for 5 min. Then, the reaction time of fluorescence recovery in the reaction of Fe3+ with ascorbic acid was investigated. Then, Fig. 3D shows the change of fluorescence intensity of fluorescent probe with Fe3+ and ascorbic acid. The fluorescence gradually recovered within 20 min, and the fluorescence stabilized after 20 min. So, 20 min was the optimal reaction condition to detect ascorbic acid.

Fig. 3E shows the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the system in the range of 4.0−10.0. As shown in the Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity of LDH-GQDs was relatively stable in the pH range of 4.0–10.0, which was attributed to the good pH buffer resistance of hydrotalcite, that increased the pH stability of LDH-GQDs. After adding Fe3+ to the system, Fe3+ quenches the LDH-GQDs system the most at pH = 7.0, and ascorbic acid has the best recovery effect on the LDH-GQDs system at pH = 6.0. The fluorescence recovery efficiency is better than that at pH = 7.0. Because Fe3+ is easy to hydrolyze in alkaline condition, and the two enol hydroxyl groups of the ascorbic acid molecule are easily dehydrogenated and oxidized to dehydro ascorbic acid under neutral and alkaline conditions. Therefore, we choose pH = 6.0 as the optimal pH value for the experiment for subsequent testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ascorbic acid and phytase
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the Fig. 4 A, under the best experimental conditions, the fluorescence detection experiment for ascorbic acid was carried out. When 180 μmol L−1 of Fe3+ is added, the fluorescence of LDH-GQD is quenched to the maximum. Then, a series of concentrations of ascorbic acid solutions were added to the fluorescent probe to “turn on” the fluorescence. With the increase of the ascorbic acid concentration, the fluorescence of the LDH-GQDs gradually increases. The fluorescent switch system for detecting ascorbic acid was successfully carried out. When the concentration range of ascorbic acid is 5−300 μmol L-1, the ratio of fluorescence intensity shows a good linear relationship. The quenching of LDH-GQD for ascorbic acid conforms to the following Volmer equation: (F0/F) = 0.9530−0.0019 Cq, and the regression parameter (R2) for the linear response curve is 0.9922. F0 represents the fluorescence quenching intensity before ascorbic acid is added, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of the probe after ascorbic acid is added. Cq represents the concentration of ascorbic acid. The LOD is 1.73 μmol L-1, which is calculated based on 3σ/K, σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal, and K is the slope of the response curve.

Similarly, the established “off-on” fluorescent technology was carried out to detect phytase. Phytase hydrolyzes l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate to produce ascorbic acid. Based on this, a fluorescent switch system sensitive to phytase activity was constructed. Under the best experimental conditions, a series of concentrations of phytase were detected. When the concentration range of phytase is 0.1–4 U mL−1, the ratio of fluorescence intensity shows a good linear relationship (Fig. 4D). The LDH-GQD for phytase quenching satisfies the following Stern-Volmer equation: (F0/F) = 0.9650−0.1559 Cq. The regression parameter (R2) for the linear response curve is 0.9948. F0 and F respectively represent the fluorescence quenching intensity before and after adding phytase. Cq represents the concentration of phytase. The LOD of phytase is 0.025 U mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of quenching of LDH-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of LDH-GQDs by Fe3+ was studied. In the Fig. 5 A, the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of Fe3+ solution and fluorescence spectrum of LDH-GQD is observed. The fluorescence excitation peak of LDH-GQD is at 350 nm and the fluorescence excitation peak is at 454 nm. The overlap ratio of the ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of Fe3+and the fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of LDH-GQD is very small. Therefore, the probability of the quenching mechanism being the fluorescence energy resonance transfer and the internal filtering effect is very small. We measured the fluorescence lifetime of LDH-GQDs before and after adding quencher (Fig. 5B and C). In the presence and absence of Fe3+, the average fluorescence lifetime of LDH-GQDs is 6.45 ns and 1.21 ns, respectively. The change in the average fluorescence lifetime is very significant. In the excited state of LDH-GQDs, electrons in the excited state can enter the unfilled d orbital of Fe, thereby causing fluorescence quenching of the LDH-GQDs. Therefore, it is considered that the fluorescence quenching of LDH-GQD should be the electron transfer and dynamic quenching mechanism [34–36].

Next, the fluorescence “turn on” mechanism of probe was investigated. From Fig. 5D, when ascorbic acid is added to the fluorescent probe reaction system of LDH-GQDs, ascorbic acid is oxidized and Fe3+ is reduced to Fe2+, thereby restoring the fluorescence of LDH-GQDs. The figure shows the UV spectra of LDH-GQDs, Fe3+, ascorbic acid, LDH-GQDs + Fe3++ascorbic acid. It can be seen from the figure that when ascorbic acid is added, the ultraviolet absorption peak of Fe3+ at 295 nm disappears, indicating that ascorbic acid has basically completely reduced Fe3+.

L-ascorbyl-2-phosphate is a compound that esterifies hydrogen on enol. Phytase can hydrolyze l-ascorbyl-2-phosphate to produce ascorbic acid and phosphate. As mentioned above, phytase can be further detected by the reaction of ascorbic acid with the system.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of this method for ascorbic acid detection, amino acids, organic acids and irons in many common foods as interfering substances of this method were determined (Fig. 6 A). Under the same experimental conditions, the ascorbic acid and the interfering substances were added to study whether they can restore the fluorescence intensity of the system. It was found that ascorbic acid had good recovery ability to fluorescent probe; glutathione, glycine, serine, proline, tryptophan, histidine, salicylic acid, alanine, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Al3+ and Mg2+ basically has no ability to restore the fluorescence of the probe. Tartaric acid, malic acid and citric acid had very weak recovery ability to fluorescent probe, which is due to the structure of the above three kinds of acids making them have a certain reducibility.

In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the system for detecting phytase, a series of common amino acids and irons in feed as an interfering substance of this method were determined. Under the same experimental conditions, the opening effects of phytase and various interfering substances on the fluorescence were tested. It can be seen from Fig. 6B that the interfering substances do not significantly interfere with the system. Therefore, the fluorescence system has a good selectivity to ascorbic acid and phytase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of this method in real sample detection
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the applicability of fluorescent probes in actual samples, we purchased fruit juice and feed samples. The ascorbic acid content in fruit juice and phytase in feed were analyzed. The concentrations of ascorbic acid in grape juice, apple juice, lemon juice and orange juice were 42.3 μmol L−1, 42.1 μmol L−1, 57.8 μmol L−1 and 86.1 μmol L−1, respectively. The difference between the test result and the data on the fruit juice packing label is no more than 5 %. The phytase was detected with the concentration of 0.14 U mL−1 in the fish feed. Then, we carried out the standard recovery experiment with adding a series of concentration of ascorbic acid (50 μmol L−1) and phytase (0.2 U mL−1) to the fruit juice and feed samples, respectively (Tables 1 and 2
). The recoveries of the experiment were 96.3 %–103.1 % and 95.4 %–104.9 % for ascorbic acid and phytase, respectively. The corresponding RSD were 2.3 %–4.5 % and 2.1 %–4.9 % for detecting ascorbic acid and phytase, respectively. The above results show that the analysis method has reliable precision and accuracy.

Table 3 shows that this method was compared with some other ascorbic acid detection methods. Fluorescence method compared with liquid chromatography methods does not need longer separation time and consumes a lot of organic reagents. It can be seen that there are some fluorescence and electrochemical methods for the determination of ascorbic acid. Compared with those methods, the method in this study has lower detection limit. In addition, the introduction of LDH improves the optical stability of the fluorescent probe and the fluorescent probe easier to store.

As mentioned in the introduction, there are also some phytase detection methods reported in the literatures. However, most of these studies do not give the information about the analytical methodology data including linear range, detection limit and recovery for phytase. Therefore, there is no table for comparison of phytase detection methods. Liu et al. synthesized quaternary ammonium salt of tetraphenylethylene and used it as a fluorescent probe to detect phytase by aggregation-induced emission [47]. Mak et al. used an electrochemical method to detect phytase, and the linear range obtained was 0.5−6 U mL−1, and the detection limit was 0.102 U mL−1 [11]. In comparison, our work is more sensitive for phytase detection with the LOD of 0.025 U mL−1. In a word, this method has the advantages of rapidity, high sensitivity, high selectivity, good stability, easy repetition.

In our work, we have developed a fast and selective method for the detection of ascorbic acid and phytase. This method is based on the “off-on” fluorescent probe constructed by LDH-GQDs. The preparation conditions are simple and convenient. The GQDs has uniform structure and good fluorescence stability by the introduction of LDH. The quenching mechanism of Fe3+ for fluorescent probe may be dynamic quenching and electron transfer effect, and the fluorescence recovery of ascorbic acid for fluorescent probe is based on redox reaction. The synthesized fluorescent probe material has been successfully used in the determination of ascorbic acid in fruit juice and phytase in feed. In the future, we will continue to develop more extensive application prospects of this material.
Han Shi: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Ligang Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Na Niu: Data curation, Software, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (B2008001), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572017EB08), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team), Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund (LBH-Z16009), and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M591501, 2017T100218).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Han Shi is a graduate student at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are fluorescence probes and molecular imprinting techniques.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications.
